did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a convolutional neural network (CNN) implemented in the Moleanalyzer-Pro system (based on a modified GoogleNet Inception v4 architecture) to assess dermoscopic images of nevi and compute malignancy scores. This AI method was used as part of the experimental evaluation to determine the impact of UV irradiation on diagnostic performance in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and describes a 'Prospective, controlled trial' involving 50 patients. It details original experimental methods (UV irradiation of nevi, EIS and CNN assessments), results with statistical analysis (p-values, confidence intervals), and conclusions derived from new data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: UV Irradiation of Nevi: Impact on Performance of Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy and a Convolution Neural Network
brief_summary: This prospective controlled trial investigated whether UV irradiation of nevi influences the diagnostic performance of electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and a convolutional neural network (CNN). The study assessed 100 common nevi in 50 patients undergoing phototherapy, measuring EIS scores using the Nevisense device and CNN malignancy scores using the Moleanalyzer-Pro system before and after UV exposure. While EIS scores significantly increased in nevi exposed to selective UV phototherapy, the CNN scores remained stable with no significant differences before and after irradiation. The results indicate that the tested EIS system is sensitive to transient melanocytic activation induced by UV light, whereas the CNN-based assessment is more robust against such environmental changes. This suggests that CNN-derived dermoscopic analysis may offer greater reliability for skin cancer screening during periods of intermittent UV exposure compared to EIS.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused exclusively on 100 common nevi in 50 patients. These were benign lesions without signs of malignancy, serving as the ground truth for evaluating diagnostic performance. Malignant lesions were excluded from the study population.

Malignant melanoma is the diagnostic target class for the EIS and CNN systems (differentiating benign from malignant). However, no actual malignant lesions were included in the study dataset; the study only assessed benign nevi to evaluate false positive rates and robustness.
The EIS system differentiates between benign (EIS-negative) and malignant (EIS-positive) lesions. The study assessed the rate of correct benign diagnoses (true negative rate) for the included nevi.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (benign vs. malignant) of nevi
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates the diagnostic performance of a CNN (Moleanalyzer-Pro) and EIS system for classifying nevi as benign or malignant. The CNN computes malignancy scores (0-1) with a predefined threshold of >0.5 for malignancy. The EIS system uses a validated cut-off of <4 vs ≥4 to differentiate EIS-negative (benign) from EIS-positive (malignant) lesions. The primary outcome involves assessing the stability of these diagnostic classifications (true-negative rates, class changes) before and after UV irradiation.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Controlled Trial Cohort (University of Heidelberg)
datasets_used.description: A prospective, controlled trial cohort consisting of 50 patients undergoing elective phototherapy (selective UV phototherapy, UVA1, SUP/UVA1, or PUVA) for inflammatory skin diseases. The study included 100 common nevi (2 per patient) from these patients. Participants were at least 18 years old. Nevi were required to be between 2-20 mm, located on intact skin without scarring/fibrosis/inflammation, and not in hair-covered areas or special anatomic sites (acral skin, mucosa). Only common nevi without signs of malignancy were included. The diagnosis of benign nevus (ground truth) was based on expert consent (JKW, HAH, CF).

use.testing: The dataset was used to evaluate the diagnostic performance (specificity/true-negative rate) and robustness of Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy (Nevisense) and a Convolutional Neural Network (Moleanalyzer-Pro) against UV irradiation. It served as the experimental dataset to measure changes in EIS and CNN scores before and after UV exposure.
use.other: The dataset was used to assess the reproducibility of EIS measurements (via paired measurements) and to analyze the impact of different phototherapy types (SUP, UVA1, PUVA) on diagnostic scores.

statistical_methods_used.method: Descriptive statistics (frequency, mean, confidence intervals, range)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize patient characteristics and EIS/CNN scores.
To assess statistical significance of changes in EIS and CNN scores from visit 1 to 2 (intralesional differences) and between timepoints for patients with measurements from all 3 visits.
To assess statistical significance of changes in EIS and CNN scores from all timepoints for patients with measurements from all 3 visits.
To assess statistical significance of differences in diagnostic classifications (labeled 'malignant' vs 'benign') between timepoints.

statistical_methods_used.method: Linear mixed effects model with compound symmetry structure
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess whether UV irradiation had an effect on the difference in EIS scores at V1 and V2, comparing UV-irradiated versus shielded nevi, with baseline EIS scores, age, gender, and irradiation as fixed factors and patient ID as a random factor.
To evaluate the reproducibility of EIS measurements (consecutive measurements).
To report uncertainty for mean EIS scores, CNN scores, and regression model estimates.
To determine statistical significance for all hypothesis tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon signed rank test, Friedman test, McNemar test, and linear mixed effects model with compound symmetry structure.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in EIS or CNN scores before and after UV irradiation; there is no significant difference in diagnostic classifications (benign vs. malignant) before and after irradiation; and UV irradiation has no effect on the difference in EIS scores between irradiated and shielded nevi.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in EIS or CNN scores before and after UV irradiation; there is a significant difference in diagnostic classifications before and after irradiation; and UV irradiation significantly affects the difference in EIS scores between irradiated and shielded nevi.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using P < 0.05. The study found no significant differences in overall EIS scores or CNN scores before and after irradiation for the full cohort. However, subgroup analysis revealed a significant increase in EIS scores for nevi exposed to selective UV phototherapy (SUP). CNN scores remained robust with no significant changes. The linear mixed effects model indicated that irradiation was not a significant predictor of EIS score differences when comparing irradiated versus shielded nevi.

Prospective Controlled Trial Cohort (Uni.metric: EIS Scores (Nevisense) and CNN Malignancy Scores (Moleanalyzer-Pro)
Prospective Controlled Trial Cohort (Uni.notes: EIS scores showed no significant overall change before and after UV irradiation for the full cohort. However, in the subgroup of patients receiving selective UV phototherapy (SUP), EIS scores significantly increased for both irradiated and shielded nevi. CNN scores remained stable with no significant differences before and after irradiation for the full cohort or the SUP subgroup. The EIS system demonstrated a true-negative rate of 49.3%, while the CNN system correctly identified 94.7% of nevi as benign. Reproducibility of EIS measurements was moderate to good (ICC 0.653).

architecture_used.main_model: Moleanalyzer-Pro (based on a modified GoogleNet Inception v4 architecture)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Moleanalyzer-Pro (Convolutional Neural Network based on modified GoogleNet Inception v4 architecture)
conclusions: The tested Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) system showed increased scores in nevi exposed to selective UV phototherapy (SUP), indicating sensitivity to transient melanocytic activation induced by UV light.
Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) results were more robust against UV exposure, showing no significant differences in malignancy scores before and after phototherapy.
The study suggests that CNN-based dermoscopic analysis may offer greater reliability for skin cancer screening during periods of intermittent UV exposure compared to EIS.
important_remarks: The study population consisted exclusively of 100 common benign nevi in 50 patients; no malignant melanomas were included, limiting the assessment of sensitivity and true positive rates.

The EIS system demonstrated a low true-negative rate (specificity) of 49.3% for benign nevi, which is lower than the 96.6% sensitivity reported in pivotal studies but consistent with known limited specificity of EIS.

In the SUP subgroup, EIS scores significantly increased for both UV-irradiated nevi (P=0.044) and UV-shielded nevi (P=0.033), suggesting that systemic or paracrine mediators from irradiated skin may affect impedance measurements in neighboring shielded skin.

The linear mixed effects model found that direct UV irradiation was not a significant predictor of EIS score differences when comparing irradiated versus shielded nevi (P=0.254), indicating the observed EIS changes may be non-specific to direct UV exposure.

CNN scores remained robust with no significant changes before and after UV irradiation (P=0.995 for irradiated nevi), suggesting superior reliability of dermoscopic AI over EIS during intermittent UV exposure periods.
Ground truth for benign diagnosis was based on expert consent rather than histopathology, as nevi were not excised, which is a limitation for definitive diagnostic validation.
The study was conducted on patients undergoing phototherapy for inflammatory skin diseases, which may introduce confounding factors related to the underlying skin conditions or medications.

EIS reproducibility was assessed with an intra-class correlation coefficient of 0.653, indicating moderate to good reliability, but 30.2% of paired EIS measurements showed class changes (<4 vs ≥4), highlighting measurement variability.
The CNN model (Moleanalyzer-Pro) achieved a correct benign diagnosis rate of 94.7%, significantly outperforming the EIS system's 49.3% true-negative rate in this specific benign-only cohort.
